title: ForeColor assigned from if-expression that can return null
description: Assigning .ForeColor = if ($condition) { $colorA } else { $colorB } is dangerous in PowerShell 5.1 because if the condition expression throws (e.g. accessing a property on a null object under StrictMode), the if-expression returns $null, which cannot be assigned to System.Drawing.Color. This produces 'Cannot convert null to type System.Drawing.Color'. The pattern occurs in Show-CronAiAthonTool.ps1 at lines 766 and 976, and could exist in any WinForms control property assignment using inline if-expressions.
detection_method: Regex scan for '.ForeColor = if' or '.BackColor = if' or any WinForms Color/Font/Size property assigned from an if-expression.
preventionRule:
1. Never assign WinForms value-type properties (Color, Font, Size, Point) directly from if-expressions.
2. Always pre-evaluate into a variable and null-check before assigning to value-type properties.
3. Related to P022 (null guard before method calls) but specific to value-type property setters.
